params.text:
When a head injury is an emergency

Most concussions are not life-threatening, but some head injuries are a medical emergency. Bleeding or swelling inside the skull can build up and press on the brain, and this can become life-threatening quickly. The warning signs of a serious injury are known as red flags. If you see any one of them, this is no longer something to watch and wait on — it is time to get emergency help by calling 999.

  • Serious risk: bleeding or swelling inside the skull can be life-threatening.
  • Red flags: specific warning signs that mean an emergency.
  • One is enough: a single red flag means you call 999 at once.

params.alt: Illustration for When it's an emergency

params.text:
Why speed matters

When something is bleeding or swelling inside the head, every minute counts. Early treatment in hospital can be life-saving, but only if the person gets there quickly. This is why you must never talk yourself out of acting because the injury “looked minor” or the person “seemed okay” at first. A serious bleed can develop after what looked like a small bump, particularly in older people and those on blood thinners.

You do not need to be certain, and you are not expected to work out exactly what is wrong. Your job is to recognise a red flag and respond. If in any doubt, treat a head injury as serious and get urgent help — it is always better to call 999 and be reassured than to wait while a treatable emergency gets worse.

params.question: Why should a head injury with a red-flag sign be treated as an emergency?

answers.text: Because bleeding or swelling inside the skull can be life-threatening, and early hospital treatment can save a life.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Red flags can mean a serious bleed or swelling, and getting help quickly can be life-saving.

answers.text: Because the person will be embarrassed if you do not make a fuss.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — a red flag is treated as an emergency because of the real risk to life, not for appearances.

answers.text: Because head injuries are never serious and always settle on their own.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — some head injuries are very serious, which is exactly why red flags need an emergency response.

answers.text: Because you must diagnose the exact injury before doing anything.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — you are not expected to diagnose; you recognise the red flag and call 999 straight away.
